sentences.text: From the ghetto, when they [INAUDIBLE] the ghetto, we marched from the ghetto police yard to a [? sighting ?] in a subgroup of [INAUDIBLE], [?
Alexept ?].
And there were cattle trucks waiting for us.
And we were loaded.
And we start moving at night.
The train start moving at night.
And we travelled all night.
And the next morning, we arrive some German's cities or towns.
We saw the German flags and people walking around in Germany somewhere.
We got no food.
The train didn't stop.
And after a few days, the train stopped.
And the doors opened from the trains.
Those trains, cattle-- what do you call it-- cattle trucks, there are no windows, nothing, just a few openings on top to see through.
And the Germans arrived.
And they say woman and children, [GERMAN].
Women and children must leave the train, to disembark from the train.
Children went out of the train.
My sister, bless her, went out of the train.
I and my brother, Chaim, were in the train facing not the door, but right in the back.
And the Germans came to every door of the train.
[SPEAKING GERMAN] Everybody answered yes.
They're out.
They left the train.
They closed the doors.
And a few hours later, we travelled for another two or three days.
I can't remember exactly.
We travelled through German cities.
You saw the German flags.
They were all actually Germany.
[INAUDIBLE] was in Poland.
We had no food supplies at all.
And it's unbelievable when I think back how we managed for a week to travel in filthy, dirty, smelly, stinking trucks of the trains.
There was no hygiene, no sanitation.
After three, or four, or five days, we arrived in Germany.
We arrived in Kaufering [SPEAKING GERMAN], working camp number one, Dachau.
It was a new camp, a working camp, which they brought the people there to work for the Germans.
They actually were building an airplane factory Messerschmitt in the later stage of the war.
We disembarked of these trains.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And as we disembarked the train, we had to go through the gate.
You had to take off your civilian clothing.
They gave you striped clothing, blue and white stripes, no underwear, and they gave you wood shoes.
The sole was thick wood sole and material, like a military [INAUDIBLE].
It's a hard material and shoelaces if you have any.
For me, they didn't have my size.
And I was lucky remained with my [GERMAN], my boots, which are made in the ghetto.
And I paid 5,000 mark, German marks.
And with this, I arrived in Dachau.
In these shoes, [GERMAN], I had Russian currency.
And I had three or four [? 10 ?] Russian ruble gold coins, I think three or four.
Before-- before we changed the clothing, they checked the mens, examined them, whatever it is.
They looked for gold, for jewelry, diamonds, and this.
Anyway, I got dressed in my blue and white strip, gray stripe.
And I [? went in ?] my [GERMAN], my long boots.
But I put my trouser not inside the boots, outside so the Germans couldn't see if I got boots.
And with these boots, I went through all the year in this concentration camp, Dachau [INAUDIBLE].
They asked us in our underwears our own barracks made out of masonite, 10 people to each barrack.
And as you came in, we got a number.
And we were registered by Jewish clerks.
They were mostly Hungarians.
And we got each a number.
They didn't tattooed us.
It was too late in the war.
But as you see from my photos that I had a number.
And we were housed in the [? round ?] bungalows made out of masonite.
There was one window and a-- and a door.
You couldn't walk into the door.
You have to bend yourself to climb in.
And it was a round thing, and 10 people to each bungalow, whatever they called it, to-- to each unit was there.
And they allocate where to sleep.
And maybe later in the day they gave us some bread-- I can't remember-- or some water soup.
This was the favorite food to the Jewish people.
Soup, free of anything, [? warm ?] water, not even salt.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then we went to register.
Or we registered the same day because most of the people arrived to a tradesmen because commercial people, or whatever you call, clerks, business men, didn't survive.
The-- the Germans didn't need them.
They liquidated them still in the ghetto.
Mostly came was tradesmen, medical people, and so on.
But the intellectual people didn't remain.
They were liquidated.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And they start to asking people from the registration.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This-- this Hungarian Jewish actually saved my life, Mr.
Levy. ?] Do you want to work in the camp or you would prefer to work outside the camp?
We didn't know that we have to walk five kilometers or six kilometers.
Takes an hour.
But to stay in the camp under the Germans, straight away we decided-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father decided not to work in the camp as an electrician.
So he became chief electrician for Messerschmitt factory in Germany there in Kaufering.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He had better facilities.
And what was the factory he's to start from scratch, to build a factory, to manufacture Messerschmitt planes.

I actually got a job to work in-- in the camp in this-- in this [GERMAN] camp number one to look as a-- as a messenger boy, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to be a messenger between the Germans officers, which was outside the camp, and the internal camp in-- in the camp, officers in the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First of all, as we came there, we find-- we find in the camp was another camp inside our camp, a woman's camp from Lithuanian woman and Hungarian woman.
You didn't know if they're women or they're men.
Everybody had shaved heads.
And women who have been in the camps became flatchested-- put it nicely.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I-- we were five children in this camp, three Lithuanian boys, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which I met them after the war.
And actually, two of them I met in New York when I've been.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the women felt sorry for me.
They used to give me bread, extra plate of soup-- plate, I didn't have a plate.
I had a metal tin from-- from something.
We got parcles from Red Cross a week later.
And in these parcels was tins of meat, milk, cheese.
And straight away, we start to do things with them.
The Germans didn't supply us any utensils even to get the black coffee, the imitation coffee.
And this we used to call a [NON-ENGLISH] where you keep your soup, your this, everything.
And this woman in the kitchen used to feel sorry for me.
They used to give me extra food.
And and in this camp, the chief of the camp was Dr.
Elkas, which I knew from-- from Lithuania.
He was the eldest.
And his brother-- I made a mistake.
His brother was the eldest in the camp.
And Dr.
Elkas was in charge of the hospital or clinic.
They called it in German [GERMAN], [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] is you get medical attention.
If you die, you die, nothing special.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the food was bad.
They used to wake us up in the morning, 4 o'clock to stay in the big appellplatz, where you count the people.
And we had numbers so and on, the number and a red triangle.
Some are different angles.
The Germans, kapos was in this-- in this camp.
Some had red ones.
Some had black ones.
The red ones were Communists.
That's why they were imprisoned in the camp, Germans themselves.
And the black one-- the black ones were criminals or anti-Nazi party.
They were in interned too.
But they were very bad murderers, robbers, rapists, all sorts of things.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this camp has been divided in all sorts of working groups.
One group we went to work for, [INAUDIBLE] Construction.
This was the company who built, who intended to build, these underground aircraft factory, which they succeeded with thousands and thousands of Jewish lives.
They perished from hunger or suicide.
They built this factory like an egg shape, drilled in foundation deep in the ground, put railway lines, narrow gauge, and brought sand and other things to build it up like an egg shape.
Then they constructed iron, steel bars for enforcement.
I can say this today because I myself am master electrician, master builder, electrician.
I know what it is still.
And I know what is this.
And they reinforced it five feet thick all the way to the bottom but still enforcing.
And they put pumps to pump up mixed cement to cover up the cement.
And when this was right out there are trains to take out the sand.
And it became a big factory on top, but five stories downstairs as well.
And they built it.
And they put cement afterwards.
Trains used to go into bring the raw materials.
Before they bring the raw materials, they brought in equipment, machinery form all over Europe.
I could-- could see machinery made in Germany, machinery made in Belgium, machinery made in Czechoslovakia, plenty came, especially from-- from Czechoslovakia.
They were advanced-- and from Hungary came very late, because this was already in 1944, '45.
No.
It was supposed in the middle of '44.
Middle of '44 came machinery from all parts of Europe.
And my late father lived in western [INAUDIBLE] is buried in Minneapolis in USA.
He was a chief electrician and could read the drawings in German.
And they built up a factory which manufactured Messerschmitts.
I can't remember if they ever [? found ?] them, but maybe came already prefabricated stuff and assembled it, whatever it is.
And I was working as a [? loafer ?], as a messenger boy in the main camp number one.
I was working there because I didn't want to march the six miles and six miles back to wear out my shoes.
Because if I wear out, they're going to put me on soles.
I have to have the boot shoes, the boot [NON-ENGLISH] we used to call it.
People used to get sores, bleeding.
They used to get gangrene.
They used to die like flies.
You're not only gangrene in the feet.
It used to swell up the body.
You used to get sores on the body, bleeding, didn't sleep at night, pain, screaming.
Wake up at night, used to be terrible screams.
Terrible, terrible.
I was lucky.
It made my [GERMAN], my-- my still used to be long shoes, like military boots.
And I was a rich man.
I had gold with me and I had a watch.
This what I survived with this.
And in the lager, in the main camp that I was, luckily when we came to Germany, they didn't send us Dachau main camp.
We came straight to working camp.
In the main camp, if I would arrived in the main camp, I wouldn't have survived a day.
They took away the children straight away, I heard, and they liquidated them.
Now, I came to this working camp number one in Dachau, Kaufering, Dachau, my father, myself, and my brother.
And my brother didn't have a job yet, was not [INAUDIBLE] job.
He was younger than me.
I managed to be the [INAUDIBLE] in the camp.
And my brother was not advised to go out to work outside the camp because they'll notice straight away a young child is going to work.
So he remained in-- in the-- in this camp.
After a month or so, they lined everybody up in this camp.
And a German walked around and took the numbers from sick people, young people, and children, and my brother's number too.
And after two hours, they took everybody off.
They didn't say why they took the numbers.
But we knew already why they take the numbers for.
And when my father came back from work, I told him that Chaim's-- my brother's name was Chaim-- they took his number for some reason or another.
And we can expect that he'll be called and taken to the main camp and be gassed.
The main camp itself was the first camp which was established by the Germans in 1933, first concentration camp in Germany, and had five or seven crematoriums.
Look-- my father, when he came back from work, we told him, we said, what are we going to do, we can't do nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and my father went to work the next morning.
Every day, we used to get up.
We used to stay in the-- in the appellplatz.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we used to get food, half a slice of [INAUDIBLE] bread.
Ex-- ex-army bread, you should remember the work ex.
I tell you why.
And we used to get a piece of black bologna and black kaffee, ersatzkaffee, imitation coffee.
They used to take wheat, try to burn it or make it like one brick, used to grind it, and used to make coffee.
This was for the day.
As I've been in the camp all the time, I used to get my lunch, a plate of soup, not from the Jewish inmate kitchen.
But I used to go to the German soldier's kitchen.
And I used to get macaroni, noodle, and milk soup.
But I knew it would not last long.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And people start dying from diarrhea, from infectious disease, from natural disease, from flies, from fleas, from lice.
Lice were not so bad already.
But fleas.
And you used to sit down.
And you used to scratch.
You used to bleed yourself to death.
Lice used to come.
They come in your head, next to your private parts, and under the arms.
There we used to shave every week.
The Jewish barbers, it was special barbers.
My late uncle was a barber in the camp too.
And he used to shave whatever he had to shave.
There's no extra privileges.
The only privilege was not to walk 10 kilometers a day to work outside.
And two or three weeks later, they called up those children, the 130 or 135.
They marched them to the railway station.
And as far as I know, they were cremated.
As far as I know, those days they were cremated in Dachau main camp.
But after the war, I find out they were actually sent, transported by rail, to Poland back to Auschwitz.
And there were very few survivors of them after the war.
They put up two poles a certain height.
Any child who went through those two poles went to the gas chamber, to the crematorium.
Who was bigger, taller survived.
And they kept them in-- in the camp as the messenger boy, as the cleaner boy, as this.
But these smaller guys who went through this particular outfit, particular frame, went to their death.
And-- and the winter came on in Germany.
It was cold.
And there was a really big German.
I've forgotten his name, SS man.
And one day [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was a [INAUDIBLE] alarm, was a siren.
Enemy planes overhead or somewhere.
So we went in to the trenches.
I counted myself 5,000 planes.
They were big as little flies.
And one smaller plane used to make a smoke mark, a round circle in the sky.
And they were flying very high.
You could see the steam from the engines, the smoke, vapor.
It makes a white mark.
But this little plane used to mark the position where they have to go in and bomb.
And you could see probably it was 200 kilometers away from us.
And they used to come in big planes.
And they used to bomb, 5,000 planes in one go.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some people in the camps, they make a mistake.
Why are they bombing the others.
They should bomb us, and finish.
Why must we suffer?
Don't think it didn't happen to us.
It happened.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
This day, and the Americans striked our camp was a Sunday.
And I had to work in the camp, outside the camp.
Five American planes came very low.
You could see the pilot.
And they strike the camp early morning.
There were still people in the barracks.
It was a Sunday.
We didn't work.
And it was those masonite barracks.
There were masonite barracks, and there were proper barracks which only roof sticked out form the ground.
And the roof from the ground was a thin layer of a [? salt ?] paper, [INAUDIBLE] paper, and cover up with sand.
There were 50-- 50 sleeping on this side and 50 seeping on-- on this side.
And in the middle was a little fireplace, which we-- we used to get some wood.
And we used to-- they used to make a fire before we used to come in.
It used to be a little bit warm-- and bed blankets.
And in this paper barracks were 10 people sleeping.
They didn't completely complete the camp yet.
And when the plane came, they saw petrol tankers, petrol tanks staying there.
The green, was painted green with a little exhaust pipe.
But they fly faster, they didn't see it.
[INAUDIBLE].
They saw these fuel tanks.
Because the German keep fuel tanks.
They didn't know it was a concentration camp.
Excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [DRINKS]
So the five planes came down in one row and opened the heavy machine guns.
When I came to the camp and I find the shells, were so long the shells from the gunners.
And the bullets were shot in those paper barracks form the masonite.
I think 3,000 people, Jewish people, were accidentally mistaken by petrol tanks.
And they were killed in-- in a number of seconds.
I don't blame the American army what was going on.
They could not afford to leave petrol for the enemy.
And I think I walked outside the camp.
And we came this.
And I was told all-- most of these people that were in this camp got killed.
And then we digged out big hole or two holes.
And we buried them.
And the Germans put some part of some part of-- I don't know that they called it.
And life was going on in the camp.
Food got worse.
The people who worked at this so-called [? new ?] Messerschmitt factory had learned.
[INAUDIBLE] was a big construction company.
And they employed the Jewish people to work.
If they payed for the labor or not-- but I think they paid to the German government for employing those people.
And in this factory worked Italians, Yugoslavs, and Hungarian people, not Jewish, non-Jewish people who came to work for the German government, very well paid and tradesmen.
So, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I was in-- still in this camp.
I-- it-- food, I had sufficient to survive.
But this inmate in the camp itself had nothing.
Whatever they got in the morning, they got, as I explained.
Lunchtime, you used to get a plate of soup.
It was in my metal container made from a-- like you buy jam, tea, or whatever teas.
And it was-- it was not too sweet, not hot.
You just drank it.
And in the evening, 5 o'clock, you stay in the queue.
You get the same thing.
If you were lucky, one day you get a piece of bread, special bread.
I'll tell you why it was special.
The Germans rejected it.
But this was special for all the Jewish people.
And a plate of cabbage soup-- you're lucky you find the cabbage or a piece of carrot or whatever it is.
And people dying by the hundreds in the camp.
And your camps, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] new-- new-- new arriving-- new inmates to the camp, Hungarians dressed with fur coats, with jewelry, with everything, with the suitcase, with their belongings.
They were stripped of everything.
And this was 1944, '45.
This was then when Germany had troubles with Hungarians, with the Hungarian government.
The Hungarian government in-- in those days had permission not to send out the Jewish nationalities from Hungary to Germany.
For some reason or another, I don't know what actually happened.
But then this agreement broke.
And the German start to take out the Jewish from Hungary.
And they came with families with the woman's camp and the other man's camp.
We had not too many woman, maybe 500, maybe less.
And--
